text: Hi, it's Abu Bakr Ali from Detroit. I'm going to be discussing a case here and some difficulties I encountered. This is a patient with the epiphrenic diverticulum. She's been having a bad reflux problem with swallowing.
She had a manometry done which was placed during an EGD and it was completely normal. And I proceeded with the epiphrenic diverticulectomy. She also had like the regular workup.
The surgeon opens the gastrosplenic omentum down to the left crus, then opens the pars flaccida to expose the right crus. The anterior portion of the hiatus is being exposed.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the right crus, avoiding injury to the anterior vagus nerve. A retroesophageal window is created, and pin rows are placed for retraction during the mediastinal dissection.
The surgeon performs posterior esophageal mediastinal mobilization, addressing an epiphrenic diverticulum located anteriorly, with additional mobilization towards the right side.

The surgeon navigates towards the epiphrenic diverticulum, which is adherent to the left pleura. The diverticulum's anterior location presents challenges in visualization and dissection due to its sticky nature.
The surgeon uses a third-degree camera to visualize and grasp the epiphrenic diverticulum located behind the left atrium. The diverticulum is being manipulated, and its thick wall is visible.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of a six-centimeter epiphrenic diverticulum, focusing on locating the superior portion and dissecting down to the neck. The diverticulum is characterized by thickened mucosa protruding between the muscle wall.
The surgeon dissects between the muscle wall and thickened mucosa of the epiphrenic diverticulum, revealing the anterior vagus nerve in the surgical field.

The surgeon approaches the epiphrenic diverticulum from behind, attempting to preserve the anterior vagus nerve while dissecting down to the neck of the diverticulum. The muscles of the esophagus are visibly split, and the anterior vagus nerve is pushed aside. The surgeon decides to perform an anterior vagotomy to proceed with freeing the diverticulum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to dissect the thick-walled epiphrenic diverticulum, carefully separating surrounding tissues to reach the neck of the diverticulum.
The surgeon uses cigar sponges for assistance and prepares to resect the anterior vagus nerve, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon reaches the superior portion of the epiphrenic diverticulum and releases it further. The resected portion is sent to pathology for confirmation. An endo loop is used to close the area, but tightening it causes an obstruction, leading to the use of an EGD for assessment.

So it was ingenious after all, I mean, I should probably have placed a little bit higher, but I was going to staple it, you know, regardless, but I thought this, uh, in the loop was going to be a good idea. And now my scissors are blunt and cutting this thing was just a very painful process. Of course it was the last life. I always get the last life scissors.

Again, I'm sharing all of the fun parts. And this liver kept flipping down. This DEMA stitch suture. Thanks, DEMA. Scoped again. This Epiphrenic diverticulum was full of cabbage. This lady loved eating cabbage. I don't know what that thing was, but it was just full of like these particles. I spent like thirty minutes trying to kind of clean like the mouth of the Epiphrenic diverticulum so I can put a staple line right next to it and it was just thirty minutes and it just kept coming.

The surgeon uses suction to clean the contents of the epiphrenic diverticulum, removing particles and debris. The scope is passed through the diverticulum, and the surgeon navigates towards the stomach after removing the endo loop.

The surgeon uses a lighted bougie to ensure it is not pushed into the epiphrenic diverticulum. A 48 French bougie is employed, and the surgeon attempts to clamp the diverticulum despite its fullness. The epiphrenic diverticulum is opened to remove its contents, allowing for a clear staple line without obstruction.

The surgeon uses a 60mm Endo GIE stapler to reinforce the staple line, slowly clamping the epiphrenic diverticulum. As the stapler closes, particles are visibly expelled from the diverticulum, indicating successful compression.

The surgeon removes the fat pad and performs a myotomy along the staple line, approximately three centimeters below the gastroesophageal junction. The mucosa is exposed, and the lighted bougie is visible, assisting with the procedure.

The surgeon uses a lighted bougie to assist with the procedure, ensuring not to spread too wide with the ligature to avoid mucosal injury. After removing the bougie, a leak test is performed, which is negative. The surgeon then performs a posterior chiroplasty using an O or number one Ethibond suture in a figure-eight fashion, followed by a simple suture. Subsequently, a Dor fundoplication is completed using slip knots.
